coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of combined carbon dots and ciprofloxacin on the expression level of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes and biofilm production in ciprofloxacin-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates from burn wound infection in Iran
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of International Society for Antimicrobial Chemotherapy.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of International Society for Antimicrobial Chemotherapy.
coredata.dc:creator: Pajavand, Hamid
Mobarez, Ashraf Mohabti
Barati, Ali
Nikkhah, Maryam
Delnavazi, Mohammad Raza
Abiri, Ramin
Alvandi, Amir houshang
Karimiravesh, Rahleh
coredata.dc:description:
Objectives Antimicrobial resistance and biofilm formation are increasingly significant public health concerns. This study aimed to examine the antibacterial and antibiofilm properties of carbon dots (C-dots) alone and in combination with antibiotics against biofilm-forming isolates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
Methods The antibacterial property of C-dots was investigated by broth microdilution method against ATCC PAO1 and P. aeruginosa clinical isolates. The antibacterial effect of the C-dots and ciprofloxacin combination was investigated using the checkerboard method. The antibiofilm effect of the C-dots alone and its combination with ciprofloxacin was evaluated using the microtiter plate method. Subsequently, the toxicity of each agent was tested on L929 fibroblast cells. In the end, the effects of C-dots on the expression levels of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes were determined using real-time quantitative PCR.
Results The combination of C-dots and ciprofloxacin exhibited a synergistic effect. Additionally, this compound substantially decreased bacterial growth (P < 0.0001) and inhibited biofilm formation at MIC (96 µg/mL) and sub-MIC (48 µg/mL) concentrations (P < 0.0053, P < 0.01). After being exposed to C-dots at a concentration of 1mg/mL for 24 hours, the survival rate of L929 cells was 87.3%. The expression of genes pslA, pelA, and ppyR, associated with biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa, was significantly reduced upon exposure to C-dots (P < 0.0023).
Conclusions The findings demonstrate a promising new treatment method for infections. Furthermore, reducing the dosage of antibiotics can lead to an improvement in the toxic effects caused by dose-dependent antibiotics and antimicrobial activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resistant
P. aeruginosa
Carbon dot
Shikonin
Biofilm
Ciprofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the combined effect of C-dots and antibiotics by broth microdilution checkerboard method
Determination of C-dots cytotoxicity and combination of C-dots with antibiotics
PCR for detection of pelA, pslA, and ppyR genes in P. aeruginosa
Determination of biofilm production and correlation of pslA, pelA, and ppyR in the biofilm formation
Antibacterial and antibiofilm activity of C-dots
Synergistic effects of C-dots with ciprofloxacin
Monitoring of pelA, pslA, and ppyR genes expression in P. aeruginosa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This work was a Ph.D. thesis supported by Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences and Tarbiat Modares University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antibiotic susceptibility patterns of P. aeruginosa.

(A) Abundance of pslA, pelA and ppyR genes in selected P. aeruginosa isolates by PCR method. (B) Gel electrophoresis of biofilm-forming genes in P. aeruginosa. M; 100 bp marker, L1; pslA gene, L 2; pelA gene and L3; ppyR gene.

Synergistic effect of C-dots. (A) Schematic checkerboard of P. aeruginosa growth inhibition with varying concentrations of C-dots. Each well represents a value. (B) Effects of CIP, C-dots and C-dots & CIP combination on clinical isolates of P. aeruginosa (concentration mg/ml). ** P < 0.003, *** P < 0.001 and **** P < 0.0001 indicate significant differences between C-dots and CIP alone and the synergistic effect of C-dots & CIP combination. (C) Effects of C-dots and C-dots & CIP on the formation of biofilm.

Cell viability assay of L929 fibroblast cells exposed to carbon dots (C-dots) at a concentration of 1mg/mL, the combination of C-dots and CIP at a concentration of 96 µg/mL, and CIP alone at a concentration of 250 µg/mL for a duration of 24-72 hours.

Quantitative real-time PCR analysis of genes involved in biofilm formation of P. aeruginosa. Expression of pslA, pelA and ppyR genes in response to the treatment C-dots in P. aeruginosa. Data are presented as means ± SDs. **P < 0.0023.
Sequence of primers and size of polymerase chain reaction (PCR) products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) rates of C-dots, extract, and antibiotics in clinical isolates with strong biofilm.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of combined carbon dots and ciprofloxacin on the expression level of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes and biofilm production in ciprofloxacin-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates from burn wound infection in Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial resistance and biofilm formation are increasingly significant public health concerns. This study aimed to examine the antibacterial and antibiofilm properties of carbon dots (C-dots) alone and in combination with antibiotics against biofilm-forming isolates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

The antibacterial property of C-dots was investigated by broth microdilution method against ATCC PAO1 and P. aeruginosa clinical isolates. The antibacterial effect of the C-dots and ciprofloxacin combination was investigated using the checkerboard method. The antibiofilm effect of the C-dots alone and its combination with ciprofloxacin was evaluated using the microtiter plate method. Subsequently, the toxicity of each agent was tested on L929 fibroblast cells. In the end, the effects of C-dots on the expression levels of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes were determined using real-time quantitative PCR.

The combination of C-dots and ciprofloxacin exhibited a synergistic effect. Additionally, this compound substantially decreased bacterial growth (P < 0.0001) and inhibited biofilm formation at MIC (96 µg/mL) and sub-MIC (48 µg/mL) concentrations (P < 0.0053, P < 0.01). After being exposed to C-dots at a concentration of 1mg/mL for 24 hours, the survival rate of L929 cells was 87.3%. The expression of genes pslA, pelA, and ppyR, associated with biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa, was significantly reduced upon exposure to C-dots (P < 0.0023).

The findings demonstrate a promising new treatment method for infections. Furthermore, reducing the dosage of antibiotics can lead to an improvement in the toxic effects caused by dose-dependent antibiotics and antimicrobial activity.

ce:section.ce:para: The increase in the global human population has led to a more widespread occurrence of pathogens and infectious diseases [1,2]. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a pathogenic bacterium and on the list of global priority pathogens (i.e.,12 species of bacteria with critical, high, and medium antibiotic resistance) published by the World Health Organization [3]. The threat of P. aeruginosa is compounded by its ability to attach and form biofilms on biotic and abiotic surfaces [4]. Biofilm formation protects bacteria against threats such as the host immune system, antibiotics, oxygen radicals, and disinfectants [5,6]. Therefore, once a biofilm is established, it becomes highly resilient to eradication; biofilm-forming infections require orders of magnitude higher doses of antibiotics for treatment, at concentrations that may be toxic to the patient [7]. We need antimicrobial agents that are effective against antibiotic-resistant bacteria, especially in biofilm mode, and that present low toxicity to humans at the effective dose.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have recently been identified as potential antimicrobial agents [8]. As a subclass of carbon materials, carbon dots (C-dots) are environmentally friendly and have relatively low cytotoxicity, suggesting they would be suitable for human usage [9]. C-dots lead to cytoplasmic leakage through mechanisms including ROS (reactive oxygen species) production, disruption of cell structure, and fragmentation of genomic DNA density, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth or causing bacterial death. One of the most notable advantages of C-dots is their functional groups and surface charge, which can be easily manipulated during synthesis or after modification, making them very useful in biolabeling [10,11]. The antibacterial activity of C-dots is highly dependent on surface charge and ROS production and, thus, facile control of surface charge is a powerful tool for controlling antibacterial action [12,13].

In this work, we used the root of Echium italicum, which contains the active ingredient shikonin, to synthesize C-dots. Shikonin is a lipophilic red pigment and its ester and alkanin isomers are used as food pigments. It is widely used in traditional herbal medicine because of its anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, and wound-healing properties [14]. We investigated C-dots derived from shikonin for their antibacterial and antibiofilm effects against biofilm-forming P. aeruginosa isolates, alone or in combination with antibiotics.

Forty isolates of Pseudomonas aeruginosa were collected from clinical samples of burn infections at Imam Khomeini Hospital on 17th May, 2021. The isolates were cultured overnight at 37°C on Muller Hinton Agar. The samples were identified by standard microbiological tests: Gram stain and culture, and later confirmed via biochemical assays and PCR.

The antimicrobial susceptibility patterns of the isolates were determined using the disc diffusion method in accordance with the 2021 guidelines of the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) [15]. To conduct this test, confirmed P. aeruginosa isolates collected from burn infection patients were cultured on Mueller Hinton agar along with the standard lab strains (P. aeruginosa PAO-1). Colonies were partially extracted with a loop and dissolved in 5 mL of sterile distilled water. Following sufficient vortexing, the turbidity of the samples was measured at half McFarland using spectrophotometry (a concentration at a wavelength of 625 nm that has an absorption equivalent of 0.08–0.13). The bacteria were cultured on Mueller Hinton agar using a sterile swab. Antibiotic discs were placed on the surface of the agar at select intervals and incubated at 37°C for 18 hrs. The diameter of the clearing ring was measured to determine the resistance of each isolate to the antibiotics of interest. The antibiotics administered were amikacin, meropenem, levofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin. The selection of antibiotics was based on the patient's clinical condition, the severity of the infection, and underlying medical conditions. All antibiotics were stored in sealed containers at 4°C.

Biofilm formation was quantified using the crystal violet method [16]. Antibiotic-resistant isolates were cultivated in MHB medium for 24 hrs at 37°C. Subsequently, 20 µL of a medium containing half McFarland turbid bacterial suspension was combined with 180 µL of MHB medium containing 1% glucose to create a 1:10 dilution. The diluted solution was transferred to 96 sterile polystyrene microplates and then incubated at 37°C for 24 hours. The microplates were subsequently washed three times with PBS buffer and the attached biomass was fixed with 99% methanol for 15 mins. The microtiter plates were then stained with crystal violet (0.5%). After washing them with water, the wells were dried at room temperature. Later, the bound dye was released using 200 µL of 95% ethanol. Biofilm formation capacity was determined by measuring the absorbance at 492 nm using a microplate reader (2100 FAX STA Reader ELISA). Reference strain P. aeruginosa PAO1 was chosen for its ability to form dense tightly bound biofilms. Biofilm experiments were replicated three times.

C-dots were synthesized using the hydrothermal method from the solid form of Echium italicum root and characterized following a previously reported method [17]. Briefly, we placed the prepared Echium italicum root in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel laboratory autoclave and added 50 mL of distilled water. The next step was to heat the material in an oven at 200°C for 14 hours. It was then cooled to room temperature. The brown solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 mins, repeated three times to eliminate large or agglomerated particles. The resulting supernatant, containing C-dots, underwent further purification using a 0.2 µm membrane. The product was dialyzed against deionized water using a 500 Da dialysis bag.

After drying, the roots of Echium italicum were crushed and transferred to a percolator. Dichloromethane, a solvent with broad solubility for polar and non-polar compounds and lower polarity than other widely used solvents like ethanol or water, was used to cover the plant up to five times the root volume. After 72 hrs, the extract-containing solvent was filtered at least three times. The extracts were combined and concentrated using vacuum rotary evaporation at 40°C.

The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of C-dots was determined using the microbroth dilution method, described in the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI, Wayne, PA). Briefly, 50 µL of MHB medium was inoculated from columns 2 to 12 of 96-well round-bottom non-tissue culture plates. Subsequently, 100 µL of C-dots (stock solution concentration = 10 mg/mL) was added to the first well of column 1 and diluted 1:2 to column 10. Finally, 50 µL of suspension was inoculated into the broth; the final test concentration of bacteria was ca. 5 × 105 CFU/mL. The MIC was determined based on the lowest concentration that inhibited bacterial growth after incubation at 37°C for 24 hours.

The effect of C-dots on biofilm formation was evaluated using the 96-well microtitre plate method. To each well of a sterile microtiter plate, 180 µL of Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) containing 1% glucose was added, followed by inoculation with 10 µL of overnight culture. Next, 10 µL of C-dots from the stock was added. The microtiter plates were incubated at 37°C for 24 hours. The content of each well was removed gently and washed three times with 0.2 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.2) to eliminate the free-floating bacteria. Biofilms formed were stained using 0.5% crystal violet dye. Excess stain was removed by thorough washing with sterilized Millipore water and the plates were left to dry. Once dry, 200 µL of 95% (v/v) ethanol was added into the wells. The ELISA reader (2100 fax stst Reader ELISA) was used to measure the absorbance at 492 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the combined effect of C-dots and antibiotics by broth microdilution checkerboard method
ce:section.ce:para: A synergistic combination of C-dots and ciprofloxacin was detected. A double dilution matrix of C-dots and ciprofloxacin was briefly constructed in a 96-well microplate. Ciprofloxacin was diluted across the x-axis, while the individual C-dots was titrated across the y-axis. For titration, serial dilutions were made in MHB up to a concentration range of MIC to 1/32 × MIC. The wells were inoculated with 50 µL of P. aeruginosa bacterial suspension (1.0 × 106 cells/mL). We evaluated synergy by calculating the sum of the fractional inhibitory concentration index (FICI) using the following formula: FICI = FICA + FICB = (MIC A + B / MIC A) + (MIC A + B / MIC B). The lowest cutoff value for a combination to be described as synergistic was FICI ˂ 0.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of C-dots cytotoxicity and combination of C-dots with antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibitory effects of C-dots and its combination with antibiotics on the growth of the L929 fibroblast cell lines were evaluated by MTT assay. Briefly, the cells were suspended in a mixture of DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium) culture media (Gibco, Carlsbad, CA), and 10% bovine serum (Gibco, Life Technology, Carlsbad, CA), 100 units/mL of penicillin, and 100 µg/mL of streptomycin at a concentration of 1 × 105 cells/mL. The cell suspension was pipetted into a 96-well plate (100 µL/well) and the plate was then incubated in a humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2 until adherence. Then, the morphology of cells was observed under an inverted microscope prior to their exposure to the C-dots. The cells were treated with 15 µL of C-dots at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, its combination with CIP at a concentration of 96 µg/mL, and CIP at a concentration of 250 µg/mL. Subsequently, 10 µL of MTT solution (50 mg/10 mL PBS) was added to each well after 24, 48, and 72 hours and the plates were incubated for 4 hours. The supernatants were removed carefully, and then DMSO (100 µL) was added to each well to dissolve formazan crystals. The absorbance of each well at 570 nm was measured by using an ELISA plate reader (Synergy-2 by BioTek Instruments Inc., Winooski, VT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCR for detection of pelA, pslA, and ppyR genes in P. aeruginosa
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA of the isolates was extracted using the boiling method [18]. Polymerase chain reactions were conducted in 25 µL volumes with 10 µL of Taq 2x Master Mix 1.5 mM MgCl2, 1 µL of each primer, and 100 ng of P. aeruginosa DNA. Amplification was performed over 30 cycles after heating at 95°C for 5 minutes. The amplification was accomplished in the following conditions: 95°C for 45 seconds, 60°C for 30 seconds at the specific annealing temperature for each primer, and 72°C for 1 minute, which was then followed by 72°C for 5 minutes. Polymerase chain reaction products were analysed via electrophoresis on a 1% agarose gel at 100 V for 50 minutes. The gel was then stained with Gel Red, followed by photography and analysis on the gel documentation system (Bio-Rad, Singapore). The primer sequence and PCR product sizes are shown in Table 1 .

The expression levels of pelA, pslA , and ppyR genes in P. aeruginosa were determined by Rivers Transcriptase quantitative real-time PCR (RT-qPCR). The primers used for RT-qPCR are listed in Table 1. Total RNA was extracted from the harvested cells using an RNA extraction kit (Yektatajhiz, Tehran, Iran) and treated with RNase-free DNase (Yektatajhiz) according to the manufacturer's instructions. The RNA concentration was measured using spectrophotometry with a NanoDrop instrument. Subsequently, cDNA synthesis was performed using the cDNA Synthesis Kit from Yektatajhiz, located in Tehran, Iran. We heated 13.5 µL samples consisting of total RNA and random hexamers, oligo (dT), which were designed to anneal to target mRNA before the start of the PCR product, at 70°C for 5 minutes to denature the RNA. Subsequently, we chilled the samples on ice for 5 minutes and added 5x first-strand buffer, dNTPs, RNasin, and M-MLV (Moloney Murine Leukaemia Virus) to a total volume of 20 µL containing: 5µg total RNA, 1 µL random hexamers, 1µL oligo (dT), 4 µL 5x first-strand buffer, 1 µL dNTPs, 0.5 µL RNasin (40U/µl), and 1µL M-MLV. The random hexamer-primed samples were incubated for 60 minutes at 37°C. Finally, the reaction was terminated by heating at 70°C for 5 minutes. Real-time PCR was carried out using an YTA SYBR Green real-time PCR Master Mix 2x in a Light Cycler 96 Roche (Germany, Hilden, Corbett Qiagen). The protocol began with an initial denaturation step at 95°C for 5 minutes, after which there were 40 cycles consisting of 30 seconds at 94°C, 10 seconds of annealing at 60°C, followed by 30 seconds at 72°C, and finally an extension at 72°C for 5 minutes. A reference gene (secA; using a single reference gene falls outside the accepted gold standard practice) was used for normalizing the expression of pelA, pslA, and ppyR genes in all samples. The expression levels of the genes were determined and compared with those of the wild-type strains.

One-way ANOVA and t test were conducted to analyse the data using GraphPad version 9.00. The normal distribution of the data was examined through the application of the Shapiro-Wilk test. Statistically significant results were indicated as those with a P value <0.05.

The antibiotic susceptibility patterns of the isolates were determined by the disk diffusion method according to CLSI guidelines. The resistance of P. aeruginosa isolates to antibiotics is shown in Figure 1 . The resistance of P. aeruginosa strains for meropenem, levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and amikacin was 50%, 20%, 27.5%, and 30%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of biofilm production and correlation of pslA, pelA, and ppyR in the biofilm formation
ce:section.ce:para: Among the P. aeruginosa isolates, 27.3% exhibited strong biofilm formation and 72.7% showed a moderate biofilm formation in a microtiter plate biofilm model. The most frequently detected gene in biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa was pelA (observed in 100% of isolates), followed by ppyR (91%) and pslA (81.8%) (Fig. 2 ). Our data suggests significant involvement of the pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes in the formation of biofilm (P < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antibiofilm activity of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized C-dots exhibited antibacterial activity against P. aeruginosa. At a concentration of 64 µg/mL, C-dots inhibited the P. aeruginosa lab strain, ATCC PAO1. The MIC of C-dots was found to be 1mg/mL for a ciprofloxacin-resistant clinical isolate of P. aeruginosa with a strong biofilm. Also, the MICs of Echium italicum root extract and ciprofloxacin were evaluated. A summary of the antimicrobial tests is shown in Table 2 . C-dots at both MIC (1mg/mL) and sub-MIC concentrations (0.5mg/mL) demonstrated significant inhibition of biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa clinical isolates (P < 0.013, P < 0.014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effects of C-dots with ciprofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: The checkerboard method showed that a concentration of 32 µg/mL of C-dots had a synergistic effect with ciprofloxacin (CIP) at a concentration of 64 µg/mL. (Fig. 3 A). The synergistic effects of combining C-dots with CIP significantly reduced the growth of P. aeruginosa clinical isolates that were previously exposed to CIP or C-dots alone (Fig. 3B) (P < 0.0001). In contrast, at both the MIC (96 µg/mL) and sub-MIC (48 µg/mL) concentrations, the combination of CIP and C-dots showed significant inhibition of biofilm formation (P < 0.0053, P < 0.01) (Fig. 3C).

Fluorescence and UV-visible measurements of C-dots were studied, showing that the emission maximum (λem) is at 455 nm when an excitation wavelength (λex) of 360 nm is used for C-dots. The TEM image shows the size and shape of the C-dots; the morphology of the C-dots was almost spherical. The mean diameter of the C-dots was estimated as 3.4 ± 0.5 nm. A C-dots was observed to be a nano-sized particle of ca. 5.43 nm to 10 nm in diameter. The surface functional groups of the synthesized C-dots were investigated using FTIR spectra. The peaks at 3200 to 3550 cm−1 and 2800 to 2950 cm−1 can be attributed to the C-OH and C-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The stretching and bending vibrations of the C=O band of carboxyl, carbonyl, and amide groups and the peak at 1280 cm−1 indicate the C-NH-C group. The peaks around 1100 cm−1 indicate the presence of the C-O group (hydroxyl, ester, epoxide, or ether). The presence of these functional groups predicts excellent water solubility of these nanomaterials without the need for further surface modification.

We exposed L929 fibroblastic cells to different concentrations of C-dots and to a combination of C-dots and CIP to evaluate their cytotoxicity. MTT assay was performed to assess cell viability. Cell viability was quantified after exposure to 1 mg/mL C-dots, and incubated for 24, 48, and 72 hours. The viability percentages were 87.3%, 72.1%, and 67.8%, respectively (P < 0.04) (Fig. 4 ). However, the percentage of cell viability decreased by 98% after exposure to CIP at 250 µg/mL for 72 hours. Furthermore, cell viability was evaluated after being exposed to the combination of C-dots and CIP for 24, 48, and 72 hours, which decreased to 56.5%, 28.3%, and 18.7%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of pelA, pslA, and ppyR genes expression in P. aeruginosa
ce:section.ce:para: The RT-qPCR method was employed to determine the effect of C-dots on the expression of genes associated with biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa. The expression of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes decreased significantly by 2.46, 2.3, and 2.66-fold after exposure to C-dots, respectively (P < 0.0023). However, no significant difference was observed between the expression levels of these three genes in the C-dots-exposed isolates (Fig. 5 ).

P. aeruginosa has the capability to adhere to and create biofilms on live tissue, implants, and catheters [19]. We evaluated the resistance of our clinical P. aeruginosa isolates to antibiotics and found that meropenem had the highest resistance rate at 50%, while levofloxacin had the lowest at 20%. Carbapenems are typically used as a last line of treatment for resistant infections [20]. In a separate study carried out in Iran, the resistance of P. aeruginosa isolates to the antibiotics meropenem, levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, and amikacin was found to be 15%, 23.75%, 20%, and 12.5%, respectively [21]. KVS Hari Kumar demonstrated that 80% of P. aeruginosa isolates were resistant to meropenem. Several other studies have indicated a similar trend. Nevertheless, a few authors have reported complete resistance (100%) to carbapenems, which contrasts with our findings. Carbapenem resistance may be primarily caused by the production of beta-lactamase enzymes. Moreover, genetic, environmental, and clinical factors are some of the influential factors in the development of antibiotic resistance. Geographical variation, sample size, and patient population may also be effective [20]. Studies have shown varying rates of prevalence of MDR P. aeruginosa strains across different regions of Iran. The prevalence in Tehran was 100%, whereas, in Bandar Abbas and Zahedan, it was 27.8% and 16%, respectively [21]. The variation could be attributed to the origin of the collected bacteria and the geographical distance between the samples. Fluoroquinolones are one of the most crucial antimicrobial agents employed for various infections. The frequency of resistance to ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin in our data was 27.5% and 20%, respectively, which was similar to the results of a previous study conducted in Iran. However, the findings contrast with Cheol-InKang and Kumar's reports, where the prevalence of ciprofloxacin resistance was as high as 96% and 67%, respectively [21–23].

C-dots were extracted for the first time from the roots of Echium italicum through the hydrothermal method. Like other C-dots, the prepared nanoparticles showed an emission property dependent on excitation. Typically, C-dots show an excitation-dependent photoluminescence that is completely different from other luminescence materials [24]. Our study utilized the L929 fibroblast cell line to investigate cytotoxicity. These cells were chosen because of their crucial role in regulating extracellular matrix circulation and wound healing. Furthermore, these fibroblasts are specialized cells that exhibit a contractile phenotype through smooth muscle actin expression [25]. The results of the cytotoxicity study demonstrated a 13.7% decrease in L929 fibroblast cell viability after 24 hours of exposure to a concentration of 1 mg/mL of C-dots. Furthermore, cell survival rates were significantly higher when exposed to the combination of C-dots and CIP at 96 µg/mL, in contrast to exposure to CIP alone at 250 µg/mL. The dose-dependent toxic effects of antibiotics can be minimized by reducing their dosage. Two hypotheses could explain the higher biocompatibility of C-dots. The first hypothesis proposes that the antioxidant activity of nanoparticles improves biocompatibility because of the direct correlation between higher oxygen content. Thus, C-dots may protect cells from oxidative stress and maintain their viability [26]. The second hypothesis suggests that the low presence of epoxy functional groups (C-O-C) results in increased oxidation potential and, consequently, greater toxicity. Furthermore, it has been suggested that the toxicity of C-dots may be linked to their synthesis protocol [27]. Silva et al. found that C-dots synthesized using the hydrothermal method had significantly higher cell viability after 24 hours. This may be attributed to an increase in metabolic activity. However, it was demonstrated using trypan blue cell counting that the synthesized C-dots have an effect on cell growth. After 48 hours, a decline in cell viability was observed, particularly in the case of the C-dots synthesized by the microwave method [28]. In addition, the carbon quantum dots synthesized by sun-exposure exhibited a cell viability rate of over 98% after 24 hours, which was comparable to the findings of our study [29].

Furthermore, several studies have discovered that C-dots show potential as an effective antibiotic alternative, exhibiting inhibitory effects on a wide range of microorganisms [30]. In our work, clinical isolates were separated from burn patients and were able to produce biofilms. C-dots at a concentration of 1 mg/mL completely prevented the growth of P. aeruginosa. However, when C-dots were combined with CIP, the MIC was significantly reduced. This provides an advantage when compared to clinically used antibiotics. It has been shown that C-dots, because of their oxygen-rich groups in surface structure, are easily absorbed by the bacterial cell wall and can enter the cell through diffusion, potentially improving its antimicrobial performance [31]. The Echium italicum-derived C-dots likely act by generating ROS and fragmenting genomic DNA, subsequently inhibiting bacterial growth or causing bacterial death. Zhenhui Kang et al. showed that C-dots extracted from cigarette smoke possess broad-spectrum antimicrobial activities. They showed that this antibacterial property could be due to the destruction of the double helix structure of DNA [32]. Yanyan Wu et al's study reported that the carbon dots derived from Bifidobacterium reduced the volume of bacteria by producing an extensive amount of ROS [33]. This decrease in bacterial density and increase in the penetration of ciprofloxacin led to an increase in the potential for killing [33].

We confirmed that combining C-dots with CIP has a stronger antimicrobial effect against P. aeruginosa than using either agent alone. Research indicates that combining nanoparticles with therapeutic agents improves the stability, solubility, and permeability of drugs when compared to pure drug solutions. Furthermore, the size and shape of the C-dots can significantly affect outcomes [34]. Our synthesized C-dots not only increased antibacterial activity by causing a synergistic effect with antibiotics, but also showed less cytotoxicity than CIP towards L929 fibroblast cells, which is an important factor to consider for in vivo administration. Thakur et al. showed that C-dots in combination with CIP selectively exhibited a synergistic effect against bacteria [35]. In a study, Jijie et al. covalently attached amine functionalized C-dots with ampicillin. Ampicillin-modified C-dots inhibited growth of Escherichia coli cells following exposure to visible light. This antibacterial effect was due to the combined effects of the antibacterial action of ampicillin and the photodynamic activity of C-dots [36].

Biofilms are communities of microorganisms that have a significant effect on the durability of bacterial infections and decrease susceptibility of bacteria to antibiotics [5]. Several studies provide evidence that 43% to 88% of P. aeruginosa isolates are capable of biofilm formation [37]. The variation observed between different studies could be attributed to differences in bacterial origin, including genetic characterization, isolation source, and environmental conditions. Studies also showed that biofilms develop resistance to antibiotics due to various factors, such as the inability to penetrate biofilm, changes in gene expression, reduction in the growth of bacteria, and the presence of persistent cells. Consequently, CIP-resistant isolates that produce strong biofilms may endure antibiotic treatment, resulting in treatment failure. Our findings showed that all the CIP-resistant P. aeruginosa isolates were able to produce biofilms, while 27.3% of the isolates produced strong biofilms. These results are consistent with the results reported by Zorica et al. and Rajabnia et al. that 78.3% to 97.55% of P. aeruginosa strains were able to produce biofilms [5].

The pslA and pelA genes play an important role in the formation of biofilm structure in P. aeruginosa. Therefore, mutations in pslA and pelA genes cause defects in biofilm formation. In addition, by inactivating the ppyR gene, suppression of the psl operon results in reduced biofilm formation. [38]. Ma et al. demonstrated that psl is necessary to maintain biofilm structure after attaching it to the surfaces [39]. The lack of biofilm production in the presence of these genes can be due to chromosomal mutations in different regulatory systems [21]. Our data indicates that pelA was the most observed gene (100%), playing a pivotal role in biofilm formation, followed by ppyR (91%) and pslA (81.8%). In the study by Ghadaksaz et al, the prevalence of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes was 83.7%, 99%, and 45.2%, respectively [38]. In their study of Iranian CF patients, Pournajaf et al. found the pslA and pelA genes to have frequencies of 89.5% and 57.3%, respectively, which is lower than our findings. [40]. Furthermore, Ramazani et al. observed a frequency of 65.5%, 37.9%, and 100% for the genes pslA, pelA, and ppyR, respectively [41]. Nonetheless, in a study by Hou et al., in which no P. aeruginosa isolates produced biofilm, 31% of isolates expressed the pslA gene [42]. This difference may be explained by the sources of the bacteria collected and the geographical distance. The absence of studied genes suggests the involvement of other genetic determinants in the formation of biofilms by P. aeruginosa. Moreover, in the presence of the studied genes, the absence of biofilm production may be caused by chromosomal mutations in various regulatory systems [36].

Several studies have reported varying degrees of biofilm reduction after using various C-dots [16,43,44]. The study conducted by Fu-Gen Wu et al. found that their synthesized C-dots completely inhibited biofilm formation in S. aureus strains at a concentration of 1mg/mL [45], which is similar to our findings. In another study by Sahiner, it was found that C-dots derived from cysteine have the ability to completely eradicate and inhibit biofilm against P. aeruginosa at a concentration of 25 mg/mL [46]; meanwhile, our nanoparticles inhibited biofilm formation at a concentration of 1 mg/mL.

Previous studies have shown that a threshold concentration is necessary for C-dots to deactivate the biofilm. Cell wall composition, growth rate, and intracellular polysaccharide adhesions of the biofilm vary significantly, enhancing bacterial resistance to antibiotics [5]. This report identifies the effect of synthesized C-dots on the expression of biofilm regulatory genes, namely the pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes, in P. aeruginosa. It is worth mentioning that C-dots reduced the expression of pslA, pelA, and ppyR genes by 2.4 to 2.66 times and also significantly decreased biofilm formation. It has been reported that mutations in the pslA and pelA genes can reduce biofilm formation in P. aeruginosa, thereby inhibiting EPS production and biofilm formation [47]. Liang et al. showed that carbon quantum dots derived from tinidazole reduced the expression of fimA, rgp (A, B), and kgp genes, which are effective in the formation of biofilms. Therefore, this nanoparticle has the potential to affect the self-assembly of proteins and inhibit the formation of biofilms [16]. In another study in Iran, it was observed that graphene quantum dots derived from curcumin (GQD-Cur) significantly increased the production of ROS, and decreased the expression of rcpA, fimA, and inpA genes by –1.8, –6.9, and 8.11 times, respectively [48]. Our study examined the main pathogenic traits of P. aeruginosa treated with C-dots and antibiotics in vitro. The next steps involve in vivo studies to confirm the therapeutic benefits of C-dots and their combination with CIP. Animal and clinical research collectively play an essential role in the translational process, providing valuable data on C-dots' efficacy, safety, and optimal use in vivo.

The results from this study suggest that C-dots, when used with low levels of CIP, can exhibit significant antimicrobial activity against P. aeruginosa. In addition, by reducing the required effective antibiotic dosage, C-dots can help decrease their dose-dependent toxic effects. Further research is necessary to establish the underlying mechanism of reported action and synergy. However, the synergistic effect of C-dots and CIP, and the reduced toxicity of this combination, suggests that C-dot-based drug combinations could offer an effective therapeutic approach for treating infections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was a Ph.D. thesis supported by Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences and Tarbiat Modares University.
